avalancheActivity.comment:
The avalanche risk is still considerable above around 1800 metres. Medium-sized slab avalanches can be triggered by individuals in some places, especially from west to north to east. Fractures can tear through the poorly built-up persistent weak layer. The avalanche prone locations are barely recognisable and should be assessed defensively. Occasionally
remote triggering is still possible. Take particular care in the transition from a little to a lot of snow and in the snow-covered entrance areas of gullies and bowls.
avalancheActivity.highlights: 15 to 30 cm of new fallen snow with wind on a weak old snowpack! Tricky situation for winter sports in open terrain!

snowpackStructure.comment: A bound snow layer (board) lies on a poorly built-up old snowpack. Fractures can occur within the old snowpack, which often consists of a sequence of melt-freeze crusts and angular forms or deep rime. In addition, existing weak layers (covered surface hoar) can be disturbed at the transition from the overlay to the old snowpack.

tendency.highlights:
Towards the middle of the week, clouds from a warm front will replace the sunshine and bring local rain or snow showers with a snowfall level of around 1500 metres.
The avalanche situation is only slowly improving.

weatherForecast.comment: South of the main Alpine ridge, many lowlands are still covered in thick patches of high fog, some of which reach up to 1700 metres. It may snow lightly from time to time, but nothing more than a wafer-thin layer of fresh snow is possible. Tuesday morning will also be cloudy on the southern side of the Alps, with high fog reaching up to 1500 to 1800 metres and many peaks covered in fog. As the day progresses, the upper limit of the fog will fall and some peaks will get a few hours of sunshine in the afternoon. Temperatures will be around -4 degrees at 2000 metres and between -3 and 0 degrees at 1500 metres. Moderately strong winds from the southwest.

avalancheActivity.comment:
The avalanche risk is moderate above around 1800 metres and low below. There is still a persistent weak layer problem. Slab avalanches can still be triggered by individuals in some places, especially on north to east-facing slopes. Medium-sized avalanches are possible when breaking through the weak old snowpack. The danger areas are mainly located in the transition from little to more snow as well as behind terrain edges and in the entrance area of gullies and bowls. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
On the sunny slopes, small spontaneous loose snow avalanches from very steep slopes are possible during daytime changes with prolonged incoming radiation.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Weak layers in persistent weak layers remain prone to triggering

snowpackStructure.comment: The snow cover is prone to triggering in places in higher, steeper areas in the transition to the old snowpack due to soft layers such as snow-covered surface hoar. There is significantly more snow in shady gullies and bowls than on sunny slopes. In the northern and eastern exposures, the old snowpack continues to be weakened by crusts and layers with faceted crystals and floating snow; the persistent weak layer problem remains. There is still significantly less snow than average.

tendency.highlights:
Towards the middle of the week, clouds from a warm front will replace the sunshine and bring local rain or snow showers with a snowfall level of around 1500 metres.
No significant change in the avalanche risk.

weatherForecast.comment: While the night will be starry on the northern side of the Alps, many lowlands south of the main Alpine ridge will continue to be covered in thick patches of high fog, some of which will reach up to 1700 metres. It may snow lightly from time to time, but nothing more than a wafer-thin layer of fresh snow is possible. Tuesday morning will also be cloudy on the southern side of the Alps, with high fog reaching up to 1500 to 1800 metres and many peaks covered in fog. As the day progresses, the upper limit of the fog will fall and some peaks will get a few hours of sunshine in the afternoon. Tuesday will start very sunny on the northern side of the Alps, but a few denser cloud fields will appear from the west during the daytime changes. Temperatures at 2000 metres will be between -4 and -1 degrees, at 1500 metres between -1 and 3 degrees. Winds will be weak, sometimes moderately strong from the south in the north.

avalancheActivity.comment: The avalanche risk is low. Small slab avalanches can be triggered in steep gullies and bowls in isolated places at high altitudes. Avalanche prone locations are covered by a thin layer of snow and are often difficult to recognise. In general, the risk of being swept away, falling and injury outweighs the risk of burial. However, be careful in the area of terrain traps.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Low avalanche danger with mostly little snow.

snowpackStructure.comment: There is still little snow. The snowpack is damp or crusted can form in places. In the higher elevations, however, angular snow crystals are still weakening the stability on shady slopes. Above this, in the extended eastern sector, there may also be a thin, bound layer, which in some cases is not well bonded to the old snowpack.

tendency.highlights: Thick patches of high fog will persist below 2000 metres, particularly on the southern side of the Alps. On the northern side of the Alps, these may clear in foehn-like southerly winds. No significant change in the avalanche danger.

weatherForecast.comment: While the night will be starry on the northern side of the Alps, many lowlands south of the main Alpine ridge will continue to be covered in thick patches of high fog, some of which will reach up to 1700 metres. It may snow lightly from time to time, but nothing more than a wafer-thin layer of fresh snow is possible. Tuesday morning will also be cloudy on the southern side of the Alps, with high fog reaching up to 1500 to 1800 metres and many peaks covered in fog. As the day progresses, the upper limit of the fog will fall and some peaks will get a few hours of sunshine in the afternoon. Tuesday will start very sunny on the northern side of the Alps, but a few denser cloud fields will appear from the west during the daytime changes. Temperatures at 1500 metres will be around -2 degrees. Weak to moderately strong winds from the southwest.

avalancheActivity.comment:
The avalanche risk is low. In the northern and eastern exposures, there is still a persistent weak layer problem at altitudes above the tree line. In steep terrain, avalanches can be triggered in a few places. However, even if the old snowpack is torn through, the avalanches usually remain small. In general, the risk of entrainment, falling and injury outweighs the risk of burial. However, be careful in the area of terrain traps.
Sunny slopes can release wet slides from very steep slopes.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Low avalanche danger, but persistent weak layer problems in the northern and eastern exposures!

snowpackStructure.comment: There is still significantly less snow than average. A thin layer partially covers a very variable and mostly hard or crusted can form old snowpack. There is significantly more snow in shady gullies and bowls than on sunny slopes, some of which are partly bare up to 2000 metres. In the northern and eastern exposures, the snowpack continues to be weakened by layers with faceted crystals and floating snow down to the foundations, and the persistent weak layer problem remains.

tendency.highlights: Thick patches of high fog will persist below 2000 metres, particularly on the southern side of the Alps. On the northern side of the Alps, these may clear in foehn-like southerly winds. No significant change in the avalanche danger.

weatherForecast.comment: On the northern side of the Alps, it will be starry through the night. Tuesday will also start very sunny, but a few denser cloud fields will appear from the west during the daytime changes. Temperatures at 2000 metres will be between -4 and -1 degrees, at 1500 metres between -2 and +1 degrees. The wind will be moderately strong to brisk from the south.

ALBINA.generalHeadline: Watch out for persistent weak layers and even older wind slab in places!
